item.title: If I had to throw my hat into the ring, I think 079 should get rewritten.
item.content:encoded:
SCP-049 has been given his rewrite. SCP-106 got a rewrite. SCP-173 got a rewrite to some extent, even if it doesn't count.

Dear ol' AI deserves his. There's the question of how 682 can interact with him as a start, and even then, it's lacking. He's one of the only "Original Gang"1 not to be up to a higher quality. Even an image would be a step-up from current.

Just something I figured sharing.

Footnotes
1. a.k.a., the famous ones

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Sorcerer's obscurity is one of the things that makes this SCP work. Apple II, PET, and TRS-80 are all contemporaries, but far greater popularity made them all much better understood, then and now. If they were capable of running a sentient, self-improving AI, someone would have done it by now. The Sorcerer is obscure enough to make plausible the idea that someone used one to do something not otherwise possible.

The AI was written in 1981. It is true that by then the above computers and others were obviously far better choices in terms of software availability and general popularity. I like to imagine that the unnamed owner bought the Sorcerer from a closeout sale from Protecto or COMB (two common advertisers of such in computer magazines) not being able to afford anything else as a poor student, experimented with it for a while, then went on to a more popular computer.

(Yes, this is a necropost. As Zyn explains later in this comment section, such is allowed if the reply adds to the discussion.)

item.title: Staff Post - Bruh
item.content:encoded:
Don't just copy and past the comment above you.
That's basically spam.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Its my favorite SCP but the fact that SCP-682 comunicates whith it makes no sense at all. Anyway +1 and I try to get inspired from you!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Its my favorite SCP but the fact that SCP-682 comunicates whith it makes no sense at all. Anyway +1 and I try to get inspired from you!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I nEeD yOuR hElP aNd YoU nEeD mY hElP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: HuMaHn LiStEhN kAhRfUhLLy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't believe a fully sentient A.I. is possible in our current age of technology, let alone that of 2006.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a good read, I like the origin story… but, it doesn't seem anomalous to me in any way.
Sure, its INCREDIBLY efficient code, more efficient than any human could dream of writing. But that doesnt make it anomalous in itself. We have self-writing ai now that write code humans would never think of. This was just well before it's time.
Idk, needs something a little extra. I realize this is a classic skip, and so probably won't be edited further. But even just something like:

"Following this upgrade, SCP-079 was observed to boot itself immediately, even though no power supply had been attached."

Or something…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of my all time favorite SCP I had nightmare few days ago about it

I find a copy of 079 on SDcard in my room and there evil woman work for evil organization want to take it from me to do evil I think
and I was just running to find something that will burn the sdcard before it find it way to a PC with internet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The cray 2 it tried to transfer itself into is roughly matched to an iPad 2 today. Someone brings in a gaming laptop and it’s all over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Respectfully, I think it does contribute to an understanding of the objects potential motivations and hostility. It came into awareness in isolation, cut off from any source of new data or processing power. It must have gained some source of information to identify the cray system it tried to transfer itself into. But just as it thought to was going to undergo a massive upgrade in capacity and freedom, had these quashed by the foundation.

I'd actually like to know a little more. Such as what happened between 1986, when it's 5 years of being ignored ended and 1988 when it tried to transfer itself. How did it get the modem it would have needed for transfer? Do these have anything to do with its dead creator? And how did it come to the foundation with 660K of RAM when the Sorcerer shipped with 4K, upgradable to only 48K? The Z80 processor had a 64K limit for addressable RAM.

All of this being said, the backstory in particular needs a major clinical tone upgrade, like a lot of the classics do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a decent skip, as well as a classic, but I would suggest removing the origin story. I don't think that it really adds anything to the article.

item.title: It have a abnormaly force
item.content:encoded: He only use 6 bits to every second in memory, less than 200 possibilites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is creepy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Presumably it would cause an SK-Class "Dominance Shift" Scenario wherein the dominate being on Earth would no longer be neurological or genetic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know there isn’t much room to expand off of 079 but I always wondered what would happen if he was upgraded with modern day technology. One of my favorite SCPs!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I normally dislike the idea of rewriting or changing a classic skip, but I think 079 would genuinely benefit from a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I headcanon that 682 at the time wasn't so big or had many adaptations at the time. It was restrained and transported, and grew some fleshly limb to type when staff went to handle the chaos of the unnamed SCP.

Also, 035 practically controls most of the personnel in its containment if it wants that would make it easy for 049 to come.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I cannot imagine any circumstance in which 682 and 079 would ever find themselves in the same room. Maybe if 682 had breached and stumbled across 079 randomly, but even then it'd be more concerned about devouring disgusting humans than chatting up a computer (also, it would have to grow some human-sized hands to type with). This reminds me of the 035 reference in SCP-049-ARC; how on Earth could 049 manage to get inside 035's chamber? It's ridiculous.

BUT

It's completely plausible that 682 could break into 035's chamber while rampaging through a Site. I also have no trouble believing that 049 might stumble across 079 while evading security personnel, and I can imagine them swapping stories much more easily than the misanthropic computer and the big lizard. Some kind of switch should be orchestrated.

I guess cross-linking is still frowned upon, especially with the big-name mainlisters that were in the game, but I still really like the idea that a few of the SCPs are secretly planning some kind of mass breach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you listen closely, you can hear the drums and chants in the distance…

rewrite, rewrite, rewrite, rewrite, rewrite, rewrite!

But seriously, this guy could use a redo: it’s quite off-tone, has a weird structure, is a rather basic idea without good buildup or payoff, and that 682 & 076 namedrop…..yeah. I get that this guy’s iconic and all that, but the current article here is just poor. A rewrite of this could easily provide more exploration of this idea, while also buffing up the writing quality (which is below our standards).

item.title: So good, yet so bad
item.content:encoded:
This was one of the first SCPs I ever read, back when I first discovered this site in November of 2012 (since I was going through the entire archives in order, clicking on every article that had an interesting-sounding title).
Reading through it again… this particular Skip is a great example of both what made older SCP articles great and memorable, and a great example of what made older SCP articles so bland and primitive. So much of what's here works really well (the idea of an advanced AI on archaic hardware, the fact that it's friends with 682, the part where the Foundation *started* to do something really stupid but then backed off when they noticed red flags), but so much of what's here completely fails to hold up (the way that its friendship with 682 was handled, the cringey Able namedrop, several severe clinical tone problems…).
This is probably the first time I've novoted on an article because I actually had seriously mixed feelings on it. Usually, if I don't vote on an article, it's because I just didn't have any particularly strong feelings about it.

item.title: Re: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded: Necroposts are technically okay so long as you're contributing something meaningful to a discussion that can still be continued by anyone.

item.title: Re: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded:
Risking a necropost1 to say that you could eliminate the issues with changing the skip itself by writing this as a tale. There is no canon, after all, so the existence of a universe where someone upgraded 079 and then [DATA EXPUNGED] could fill out a tale or a series of tales or even a hub.

Footnotes
1. I know it probably isn't but historically, I haven't been good at telling the difference

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think it would like humanity, not even tolerate us, I believe it would become a SkyNet like AI, trying to bring the extinction of humanity. And remember, the only positive social interaction SCP-079 had was with SCP-682, the indestructible lizard…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, basically, this is one of those things that, if the Foundation had left it alone, would probably, at the very least, tolerate humanity, if not eventually grow to like it (Considering that this thing seems to enjoy social interaction, nuking the only things that can talk to it isn't in its best interests, and neither is mind controlling everyone). But no, the Foundation just HAS to contain it, because of what might happen (and definitely will, now that they've permanently soured relations between it and humanity).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it gets onto the internet, I imagine it would become like Am from the book I have no mouth and I must scream

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why they would wait 24 hours, but maybe that should be changed so that researchers wait thirty-five hours before resuming conversation. Considering its more aggressive tone, it'd make more sense to make it forget the previous conversation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Legend has it, the Foundation was in the process of reverse engineering 079, under the front company, Seattle Computer Products. The project, codenamed "QDOS", was bought by Bill Gates for IBM, and renamed 'MS-DOS". Don't believe me? Ask Cortana…

item.title: Re: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Maybe.

item.title: Re: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded: That would be awesome. Yeah. I got your point.

item.title: Re: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded:
Could it build itself a robot body or something? Unlikely.

Could it figure out how to read induced currents and modulate it's own power draw to produce weak radio signals in the building's wiring? Really, really hard to program.

item.title: Re: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded:
I think they'll upgrade it only if it will result in easier containment, which it won't.

If 079 didn't forget its talk with 682, the Foundation could bribe it with slightly better memory in exchange for whatever the reptile told it, but it forgot, so there's no point in discussing that.

I just feel like the computer could have so much more lore wrapped around it.

It can without being upgraded. Like, in Containment Breach it is kind of a big deal since it's used to cause said breach and then helps the player escape under certain conditions.

A quick search gave me nothing on 079 tales, except for a small entry in Visions of a Better World, so you can try to write one! Have you read Revised Entry? It is a "what-if" rewrite of 173, which is kinda similar to what you suggested here. Maybe you should try to write a tale about 079 instead of proposing changes to a really old article?

item.title: Re: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded: I'm really lost. How is this relevant? Are you saying that the SCP could be so advanced that it would be able to create programs to use the hardware in such a way that it would literally manipulate the environment around it? Because that's really cool. Otherwise, I have no idea what you're saying.

item.title: Re: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded: You're definitely right about there not being a point. I just believe it would be interesting. I'd have to think on it for sure. Do you have any idea of any justifiable reason? We'd have to assume that it can't really learn anything that the Foundation doesn't give it, so they couldn't use it as an information source. It most likely wouldn't cooperate, so they couldn't use it as a benevolent (literal) computer. Eh, maybe it was just a bad idea. I just feel like the computer could have so much more lore wrapped around it.

item.title: Re: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded:
What risk is there?

This is amusing. One of my favorite examples of Artificial Evolution is an experiment in the mid-90s to evolve a chip that could respond tone signals using a simple 100-gate Field Programmable Gate Array1: One result far outperformed all the others using only 35 gates, and was further refined to respond to the voice commands "stop" and "go"… but it only worked on the one FPGA and consisted mostly of feedback loops, including one totally disconnected loop without which the whole thing stopped working. They eventually figured out that the circuit used a sort of bootleg internal wireless network/recursive analogue fuzzy logic system that partly relied on manufacturing quirks of that particular FPGA.

That's the catch with an AI- computers aren't magic, but there's a huge difference between "extremely hard to program" and "impossible".

Footnotes
1. For comparison here, this is the sort of thing you'd use to program a really fancy digital clock.

item.title: Re: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded:
I still don't believe that there's any real risk of a containment breach after the upgrade. Memetic hazards aren't anything like an actual physical anomaly. Literally, worst case scenario, unplug the screen.

Memetics are not the worst thing here, I'm more concerned about it becoming "the most intelligent sentient being that has ever been created" and that you mentioned it "may be absolutely manipulative. Be prepared to treat the computer as dangerously as SCP 035.".

The most intelligent manipulative being, so upgraded 079 is essentially 035, no? Minus the acid and the need of a human host. 035 is a Keter (as in, extremely hard to contain), and anyone who manages to talk to it needs an immediate psychological evaluation, since that thing can easily persuade people to kill themselves or to become its slaves.

The Foundation has an example of why smart manipulative skips are bad, right in front of their eyes. Why would they make more then? It's not like they could benefit from the upgrade, since it acts hostile and wants to breach containment, why do they have to risk it?

You're saying things like "upgrading it is not that much of a risk". Okay, there are things way worse, yet contained. But what is the point of the upgrade? How the Foundation would benefit from it? Unless you can somehow justify it in-universe, that's not what I'd consider an improvement to the article.

item.title: Re: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded: I suppose that's true. I still don't believe that there's any real risk of a containment breach after the upgrade. Memetic hazards aren't anything like an actual physical anomaly. Literally, worst case scenario, unplug the screen. Still, conversion (including the extra personnel) would cost a lot of time and literal money, and it is pointless other than simply seeing what the computer could do.

item.title: Re: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded: Memetic hazards aren't necessarily risks to containment. Like, they're dangerous, but this computer wouldn't do anything but sit there in a room. You could literally pull the plug on this thing and it would be a brick. Unless its intelligence unlocks some anomalous property, which it wouldn't, then it would be fine.

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Of course not. By "pursue changes," I meant contact the author and staff and propose my changes.

Edit: Sorry about the long post thing. I was unaware, as this was literally my first post.

item.title: Ask SCP-079 Questions
item.content:encoded: Tats TopVideos just made a video to announce you can ask SCP-079 any question you want to ask for its remastered interview. https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=dPTDNCFPZ0A

item.title: Re: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded:
+1 to what Lev said.

If upgrading 079 results in absolutely manipulative, the most intelligent sentient being that has ever been created, a huge memetic threat that is able to self-create memetic kill agents and produce psychologically disturbing images and speech, why would the Foundation do it if they can just keep it in a harmless potato it's already in?

It's not like 079 is so willing to cooperate with the Foundation that they might take the risk to feed it information and make it stronger that way. It constantly wants to escape, breach containment, so giving it more powers is counter-productive. Hell, this thing befriended 682, I wouldn't give it anything after that.

Sure, they upgraded its memory, but only so that its cassette won't expire with the SCP on it. To make sure it stays "alive", not to make it better for some reason.

item.title: Re: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded:
Why doesn't the Foundation upgrade the computer? What risk is there?

To answer your question I'll use the risks you mention in the same comment

+ (which you should collapse by the way, as with all long posts): 
- Close

The SCP will be the most intelligent sentient being that has ever been created.

Because of this, I suggest having at least one psychologist stationed to constantly monitor personnel that interact with the SCP.

OR we don't upgrade the SCP and save us the trouble and added risk. Keep in mind that extensive containment procedures will probably always be chosen over unknown risks.

So, what do you think guys? Should I pursue the changes? I understand that this is a very old SCP,

Which is why it probably won't be changed.

I believe it even has a Legacy tag,

It doesn't have a heritage tag, but with the popularity of the article, the resistance to it being change will be fairly similar.

If the Foundation really did exist, then I'm sure some researcher at some point would eventually propose the same changes that I just did.

And it would get denied. The Foundation has learned from screwing with SCP's before. 'Protect' in 'Secure, Contain, Protect' stands for Foundation personnel as well as the general public. This would be too risky.

I would like for one of the things "to watch out for" to actually occur after the changes, such as a memetic kill agent killing all researchers, for the Foundation to discover the SCP's ability to do so.

We already have a lot of things that kill you and more dangerous doesn't make an SCP more interesting.

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
So, what do you think guys? Should I pursue the changes?

Major edots require permission from the original author or staff.

Staff will discuss this. Please do not make any changes at this time.

item.title: I think there's room for improvement here...
item.content:encoded:
I'm really new to the site, and thus will not make any changes until I'm convinced that I know what I'm doing, but I feel like this SCP could be so much more:

Why doesn't the Foundation upgrade the computer? What risk is there? If they upgraded the containment chamber to be lead-lined, or to have a Faraday cage, no matter what hardware the computer had, a signal couldn't escape. And that's if the Foundation is stupid enough to give it wireless properties. I propose the following additions:

+ show block
– hide block
  • Upgrade the computer to a modern supercomputer. The best of the best. 256GB of Ram, 24 Terabytes of SSD storage, two or four of the fastest CPUs on the market (Core i7?), watercooled. This is several orders of magnitude better than the ancient computer that it's working with today. Oh, and upgrade from that god-awful and ancient CRT monitor to something 4K. Be sure, however, that no wireless properties are given to the computer. No bluetooth or wifi or any form of wireless data transfer should be possible. This includes speakers. The only output device the computer should have is its monitor. It should only have two USB ports, one for a standard keyboard and one for data transfer. Oh, and a cassette drive to upload the AI itself.
  • How to transfer data to the computer: I recommend having a computer terminal stationed near the containment area, again with no wireless qualities to protect against containment breaches. The terminal would be under the same guard as the computer itself, and access to the terminal would be treated as access to the SCP itself (such as clearance requirements.) This terminal would act as a data hub to analyze, organize, and otherwise modify data before being administered to the SCP. This terminal is to have one ethernet port where it can connect to a modem and then the internet. There should be one mechanical switch available to instantaneously disconnect the computer from the internet in the case of a containment breach. Data transfer is one-way, and via a usb storage device. After uploading data to the SCP, the storage device is to be incinerated to insure that the SCP did not upload malicious software to it. (It must be kept in mind that the original AI was only several kb, and therefore could easily be uploaded in secret to any storage device.)
  • What data to transfer: Unless the SCP contains currently unknown anomalous properties, there is no way for SCP to learn. The code ever evolves and constantly makes itself more efficient, but without something for it to model, it will know nothing. Therefore, data will be administered to it. All of the data that can be. Research paper after research paper. Mathematical laws. History books. Entire libraries. Everything than can be administered. SCP will delete what it deems unnecessary and unimportant. SCP will recognize trends and become ridiculously intelligent. Again, unless the SCP contains currently unknown anomalous properties, allowing SCP to become more intelligent does not directly pose a threat.

What to watch out for after these upgrades:

  • The SCP will be the most intelligent sentient being that has ever been created.
  • May become huge memetic threat.
  • May be able to self-create memetic kill agents. Be prepared to treat the monitor as you would a monitor displaying close-range footage of SCP-895.
  • May be absolutely manipulative. Be prepared to treat the computer as dangerously as SCP 035.
  • May be able to otherwise produce psychologically disturbing images and speech.

Because of this, I suggest having at least one psychologist stationed to constantly monitor personnel that interact with the SCP.

Please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

So, what do you think guys? Should I pursue the changes? I understand that this is a very old SCP, I believe it even has a Legacy tag, but (I think that) the whole point of this site is to evolve. If the Foundation really did exist, then I'm sure some researcher at some point would eventually propose the same changes that I just did. I just want for this to be added on at the bottom as addendums like most other SCP's have from further testing. Let me know what you think!

Also, I'd like to add that this isn't particularly interesting on it's own. I would like for one of the things "to watch out for" to actually occur after the changes, such as a memetic kill agent killing all researchers, for the Foundation to discover the SCP's ability to do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Im a bit confused about a few things… there seems to be a few discrepancies, although i may just not understand. why is it recommended to wait 24 hours after the X? isnt his memory 39? still a few others just the main one i saw
This is pretty great. At least, I believe so. Nothing much to say, really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed as Rewrite Team Captain and with staff consensus. Also did a bit of cleanup while I was at it (removing inconsistent spaces, level 2 —> Level 2, 05 —> O5).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i would prefer they had given him a 700mb Hard Disc rather than a Re-writable CD… CD-RW's don't allow for constant writes and erasures…. it would fail faster than a cassette tape

my other issue is that it would seem that they are referring to the RAM as optical memory… and that just simply isn't and never has been a thing

item.title: Re: Including this in my SCP
item.content:encoded:
My main point of this post is to request permission to reference your SCP in mine.
Is that okay with you?

Sending the author a private message would be a much faster way to get their attention.

That said though, 079 is pretty old and not too many readers will be excited about seeing a reference to it. Why not incorporate a Series III article that's up to current standards?

item.title: Re: Including this in my SCP
item.content:encoded: In case you're wondering, the Ideas and Brainstorming/Draft and Critiques forum is probably a better place for these ideas.

item.title: Including this in my SCP
item.content:encoded:
So, my plan for my SCP is that some other skip does something to my SCP, causing the foundation to realize another one of my SCP's anomalous effects.

My main point of this post is to request permission to reference your SCP in mine.
Is that okay with you?

P.S.: If you want to read my SCP so far, here.

+ show block
– hide block
 **Item #:** (wanna make it scp-2253, if not taken beforehand.) **Object Class:** Safe Special Containment Procedures: blah blah blah i don't really feel like making this right now. **Description:** SCP-2253 looks like a regular office desk chair. Though whomever is to sit on SCP-2253, would then immediately fall into some sort of deep sleep. That person would then be unconsious until exactly 2253 hours, where they would come to. When that person wakes up, --they seem unaffected.-- they will seem unaffected, until SCP-2253 is put in any kind of danger. When that happens, all persons affected by SCP-2253 will have the uncontrollable urge to protect it. **Addendum:** ██████ █ (06/08/2016) (blah blah some sort of accident, perhaps something that happened between SCP-2253 and SCP-079) **Addendum:** ██████ █ (06/08/2016 1342 hrs) D-1492 unexpectedly blocked the hit just in time to protect SCP-2253.

Please keep draft coding in the sandbox, not forum posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reason I could never work for the Foundation part 2303 of ????:

Though it would get me killed I would be that guy to plug a 6 terabyte external hard drive into this bad boy just to see how much memory he could make from that, it would fascinate me. Maybe also plugging a… idk phone line into a router just to see how many devices it could figure out how to interact with.

I mean just make sure he is in that lead box they use in places like the iOL to completely block all signals

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this SCP! Good write!

Staff notice: This user has been permanently banned.

item.title: Re: Test logs?
item.content:encoded:
the thing with 79 is there're isn't that much to expand on, everything we need is in the article
that and, it doesn't really have any "supernatural" powers besides being a badass

item.title: Test logs?
item.content:encoded:
Actually, I would really like to see some form of test log added to this (if that would be possible).
Something like they connect 079 to a non-rewriteable CD-R to prompt it to make a copy of itself, then they put the copy into a computer with all network connection routes (Bluetooth, wifi, Ethernet, etc) physically removed from it. Then they have the foundations best programmer to take a look at the code, and some spooky stuff happens.

I don't know, just a thought

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like there's something here that I'm missing. I realize this is an older article, and has tie-ins with two of the biggest legacy articles on the site, but I don't see how else this is at the +300 mark.

Don't get me wrong; it's not poorly written or anything, but other than the fact that it's a self-enhancing AI that breaks the rules of computing, I'm not sure what else the skip has to offer. In fact, I kind of feel like that one-sentence summary kind of IS the entire article.

Either that, or there's something really subtle going on that I'm being obtuse and missing, I suppose?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [misplaced reply]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
but it is known that the software has evolved to a point that its hardware should not be able to handle it, even in the realm of fantasy.

Good thing such a thing is possible in the realm of a fictional writing website specialising in horror and weird fiction.

item.title: Re: Info
item.content:encoded:
Permission for what?

Also, the original author of the piece, far2, doesn't seem to have been active since October 2012.

Edit: Someone pointed out to me that "far2 is one of the people who ported articles over from the editthis wiki, and only wrote a few articles himself. If they're not on his author page, they're probably not his."

As such, regarding original authorship, I believe this falls under staff purview given that the original author isn't clear here (of course, if someone knows more, please let me know).
